coredata.dc:title: Cerium-doped sulfur quantum dots for the detection of lead ions and the use of anticounterfeiting ink
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Jothi Vinoth
Rhim, Jong-Whan
coredata.dc:description: Cerium-doped sulfur quantum dots (Ce@SQDs) were prepared at low temperatures by a hydrothermal method using sulfur powder, ethylenediamine, and cerium acetate hydrate. The Ce@SQDs were spherical with an average particle size of 4.4 nm and exhibited excitation and emission maxima at 335 and 410 nm, respectively, with a high quantum yield of 48.92 %. The fluorescent Ce@SQDs showed a selective and sensitive response to Pb2+ ions with a detection limit of 8.2 nM in the linear detection range of 10–90 nM. The efficacy of Ce@SQDs for Pb2+ ion detection was confirmed through Pb2+ ion detection in real water analysis. In addition, the Ce@SQDs showed high potential for anticounterfeiting ink applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfur quantum dot
Fluorescent sensor
Metal ion detection
Anticounterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity analysis of Ce@SQD fluorescent sensor for Pb²⁺ detection
Detection of Pb2+ ions in real samples using Ce@SQDs

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (2022R1A2B5B02001422).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of Ce@SQDs, (b) excitation and emission spectrum of Ce@SQDs, (c) different excitation wavelengths of Ce@SQDs.
(a) XRD pattern of Ce@SQDs, (b) FT-IR spectrum of Ce@SQDs.
(a) HR-TEM image at 50 nm scale bar, (b) lattice fringes at 2 nm scale bar, (c) size distribution, (d) EDS analysis of Ce@SQDs.
(a) XPS spectra of Ce@SQDs, (b) S2p, (c) C1s, (d) N1s, (e) O1s, (f) Ce3d.

(a) Fluorescence spectrum of Ce@SQDs with various concentrations of Pb2+ ions, (b) resolution of fluorescence against Pb2+ ions concentration, (c) linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and Pb2+ ions concentration (10–90 nM), and (d) Fluorescence spectra of Ce@SQDs with Pb²⁺ ions and coexisting metal ions. Dark cyan bars represent selectivity assays, and the influence of competing ions is indicated by orange bars.
(a) Interference evaluation of Ce@SQDs in the presence of Pb2+, (b) Ionic Strength of Ce@SQDs, (c) Temperature conditions of Ce@SQDs (10–80 °C), (d) Storage stability of Ce@SQDs for 50 days.
Fluorescent ink using Ce@SQDs normal to under UV light.
Comparison of various Fluorescent Probes using detect Pb2+ ions.
Detection of Pb2+ in environmental water samples.

head.ce:title: Cerium-doped sulfur quantum dots for the detection of lead ions and the use of anticounterfeiting ink

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cerium-doped sulfur quantum dots (Ce@SQDs) were prepared at low temperatures by a hydrothermal method using sulfur powder, ethylenediamine, and cerium acetate hydrate. The Ce@SQDs were spherical with an average particle size of 4.4 nm and exhibited excitation and emission maxima at 335 and 410 nm, respectively, with a high quantum yield of 48.92 %. The fluorescent Ce@SQDs showed a selective and sensitive response to Pb2+ ions with a detection limit of 8.2 nM in the linear detection range of 10–90 nM. The efficacy of Ce@SQDs for Pb2+ ion detection was confirmed through Pb2+ ion detection in real water analysis. In addition, the Ce@SQDs showed high potential for anticounterfeiting ink applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Uncontrolled release of hazardous chemicals, especially heavy metal ions, from industrial, laboratory, agricultural and household sectors can cause serious problems to the environment [1]. In many developing countries such as India, negligence in wastewater treatment is another major cause of heavy metal ion contamination in water resources [2]. In particular, highly toxic heavy metal ions are not biodegradable and can precipitate in groundwater and soil, causing serious health problems in living organisms even at low concentrations [3]. Lead (Pb), one of these heavy metals, is the most widely known harmful element among metal ions. Lead is known to cause neurotoxicity and cause pulmonary embolism and kidney problems in living bodies [4,5]. Because lead contamination in food or the natural environment can be fatal and cause serious health problems, scientists are focusing their efforts on detecting Pb2+ ions in natural sources and developing new technologies to reduce contamination [6]. In general, to analyze heavy metals in food samples or environmental samples, various sophisticated analysis methods such as atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES), X-ray fluorescence (XRF), neutron activation analysis (NAA), and anode stripping voltammetry (ASV) are used [7]. In particular, Pb2+ can be analyzed using a variety of established methods, including colorimetry, flow injection, spectrophotometry, electrochemistry, coupled plasma mass spectrometry, and atomic absorption spectroscopy [8–10]. However, these instrumental analysis methods are time-consuming, require expensive equipment and skilled users, and do not allow on-site detection, which severely limits their applicability [11].

Additionally, many currently used nanomaterials have been shown to have reduced affinity for sensing low concentrations of metal ions in aqueous environments and reduced processing efficiency in large-scale processing [12]. Therefore, it is important to find ways to detect and remove these metal ion contaminants using advanced materials that are sensitive, biocompatible, and non-toxic. From this perspective, nanomaterials have improved physicochemical and surface properties, making them suitable materials for creating sensors for investigating various metal ions under aquatic conditions [13].

Meanwhile, with the advent of intelligent packaging systems, interest in developing anti-counterfeit ink is increasing. Recently, Kashmitha Muthamma et al. [14] developed anticounterfeiting ink using various carbon-based nanomaterials such as carbon quantum dots, carbon nanodots, graphene quantum dots, and carbon-generated polymer dots. Additionally, Namratha Ulla et al. [15] showed that CDs manufactured using cassava have unique optical properties when coated with an eco-friendly ink formulation. However, most of the current fluorescent carbon dot-based anticounterfeiting inks are water-soluble and lack stability, so there is a problem in that the fluorescence intensity rapidly weakens when exposed to moisture [16]. Therefore, in order to improve the effectiveness of the anticounterfeiting function without external interference, it is very important to develop anticounterfeiting fluorescent ink with water resistance [17].

On the other hand, sulfur quantum dots (SQDs), a new type of non-metallic fluorescent quantum dots, have recently attracted attention as fluorescent probes due to their excellent selectivity, fast response, high sensitivity, and excellent imaging [18]. However, delayed response time, low photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY), and extremely low S to SQDs conversion efficiency (CE) (less than 1 %) hinder the practical use of SQDs. To overcome these limitations, several methods have been proposed, including O2 acceleration, H2O2 or ion-assisted synthesis, hydrothermal synthesis, sonication, microwave reaction, and mechanochemical methods, as well as surface passivation methods to produce fluorescent SQDs [19,20]. Additionally, in order to enhance the optical characteristics of SQDs, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), carboxymethyl cellulose, hydroxypropyl-cyclodextrin, heparin, or polysodium 4-styrene sulfonate were utilized [21,22]. Although it took a long time to convert zero-valent sulfur to polysulfide in each reaction system, the method of passivating the SQD surface with polyethylene glycol (PEG), a surface stabilizer, was widely used, and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) may also be used as a passivating agent in SQD synthesis [23]. They are more stable due to their capacity to interact strongly with the SQD surface due to the presence of multiple side hydroxyl groups [24].

However, a method to functionalize nanoparticles using cerium has been proposed [25]. Functionalization with cerium is unique in that it uses simple chemical reactions at the nanoscale (Ce3+ = Ce4+ + e-) and can act as an oxygen buffer. The reduction of Ce4+ → Ce3+ initiates the generation of lattice mobile oxygen, which easily leads to the generation of surface oxygen vacancy defects and simultaneous electron formation. The oxygen defects left behind are also hot spots that allow noble metal catalysts to stick together and perform better due to the way the metal and CeO2 interact with each other, allowing photoinduced charge carriers such as electrons and holes to move more easily. Here, CeO2 acts to modify these charge carriers, which undergo a rapid recombination process [26]. Also, the use of diamagnetic cerium has the advantage of increasing the porosity of the sample [27]. This functionalization method using cerium has the advantages of being porous, having a high surface-to-volume ratio, being inexpensive, easy to make, and operating in a variety of environments. In particular, chromate has a porous structure and a high surface-to-volume ratio, making it suitable for use as a fluorescence monitor [28–31]. Chromite is very important for sensor and electronic applications because it does not react with other chemicals [32].

In this study, cerium was doped with SQDs through a simple hydrothermal method and used as a fluorescent probe for detecting Pb2+ ions. To the best of our knowledge, this study represents the first application of Ce@SQDs fluorescent probes for fluorescence detection of Pb2+ ions in real samples. The synthesized fluorescent probes were characterized using various analytical and spectroscopic techniques. A thorough mechanistic investigation was also studied to elucidate the chemical interaction between Ce@SQDs and Pb2+ ions. Additionally, the modified Ce@SQDs fluorescent probe shows excellent quenching activity toward Pb2+ ions, showing an impressive linear range and low detection limit. This investigation highlights the strong fluorescence performance of Ce@SQDs for Pb2+ ion detection. Additionally, Ce@SQDs have the potential to be used as anticounterfeiting agents due to their high fluorescence properties.

Sublimed sulfur powder, sodium hydroxide, cerium acetate hydrate, and polyvinyl alcohol were procured from Daejung Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Siheung, Gyeonggi-do, Korea). The metal salts FeCl2, Pb(NO3)2, CoCl2, MnSO4 .H2O, K2SO4, Al2O3, (CH3COO)2Ba, Cu(NO3)2, CaCl2, HgNO3, ZnCl2 NiCl2, MgCl2, and AgCl were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). The analytical grade reagents that were utilized did not require any additional purification steps before being utilized.

SQDs were created by employing the hydrothermal technique developed by Ruchir et al. [33] but with a few significant alterations. In order to do this, 50 mL of ethylenediamine was used to dissolve 1 g of sulfur powder and 0.5 g of cerium acetate hydrate. The mixture was then put in a 100 mL Teflon-lined hydrothermal reactor and heated to 60 °C for eight hours. Ethylenediamine was extracted from the reaction mixture by vacuum evaporation at 90 °C after cooling, resulting in a dense, brown precipitate. Ce@SQD powder was produced by freeze-drying a yellow solution that was obtained by washing the residue with water.

Agilent Cary-100 spectrometers from Hudson, Massachusetts, United States of America, were utilized in order to record the UV–visible absorption spectra of SQD solutions. The F-4500 spectrometer (Hitachi, Chiyoda, Japan) was utilized in order to record the fluorescence spectrum of every single sample. Measurements of photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) and time-resolved fluorescence decay curves were carried out with the assistance of an FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, London, United Kingdom). Using a Vertex 70 FTIR spectrometer from Bruker in Germany, pictures were obtained by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). These images were produced with an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 from Hudson, Massachusetts, in the United States. Different Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were utilized in order to investigate the surface groups of single-quantum dots (SQDs). An ESCALab 250 Xi spectrometer (Thermo, United States) was utilized in order to carry out X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) examinations. In order to display X-ray diffraction research (XRD), Cu K radiation was utilized on a DX2700BH X-ray diffractometer (Haoyuan, China).

To investigate the sensitivity of the produced Ce@SQDs to Pb2+, their interactions with different metal ions were observed in water. First, the Ce@SQDs were diluted 100 times with distilled water, and 200 μL of the Ce@SQDs probe was incubated in a centrifuge tube with isometric amounts of various metal ions (Zn2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Ba2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, K+, Al3+ and Fe2+) for six minutes. The fluorescence spectra were then captured at the excitation wavelength of 335 nm. To test the probe sensitivity to Pb2+, a series of Pb2+ standard solutions were added to the Ce@SQDs probe. Various gradients of Pb2+ concentration were determined using the same method as previously used, and all measurements were repeated three times at room temperature.

Test samples of tap water, bottled water, and river water collected near Kyunghee University (Seoul, Korea) were sieved through a 0.22 μM membrane mesh to isolate unsolvable impurities. The Pb2+ ions at values of 20, 30, and 40 μM were added to the water samples. The detection device was standardized at pH 8 using (tris HAc buffer solution) to determine the concentration of Pb2+. The concentration of Pb2+ in real samples was analyzed using the Ce@SQDs sensor.

Anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink was prepared by dropping 15 mL of 10 % polyvinyl alcohol into the prepared Ce@SQDs solution. The reaction was then sonicated for 30 min. After this solution was vigorously stirred with a magnetic stirrer for 30 minutes, a viscous fluorescent ink was produced. Images were written or drawn on conventional filter paper while dispensing the synthesized Ce@SQDs fluorescent ink into empty refill tubes. The ink was then dried in an open space at room temperature. The dried paper was examined under UV light (365 nm), and images of visible characters were captured using a digital photographic camera.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum absorption bands can further support the excitation-independent behavior of Ce@SQDs, as shown in Fig. 1a. The absorption peaks at 247.3 nm suggest that the luminescence might come from the sulfur core lattice. The full surface passivation and excitation-independent behavior of Ce@SQDs may contribute to their high quantum yield [34]. Ce@SQDs fluorescence excitation and emission spectra are shown in Fig. 1b. Ce@SQDs had a maximum emissions peak wavelength of 409.57 nm and an excitation wavelength of 335.9 nm [35]. The fluorescence emission spectra of Ce@SQDs are shown in Fig. 1c at a variety of excitation wavelengths (excitation wavelengths). Indicating the potential of photon reabsorption and demonstrating that Ce@SQDs possess excitation-dependent PL characteristics, the emission peak position exhibited a modest red shift when the excitation wavelength increased from 310 nm to 410 nm. This shift was observed in the emission peak location. The fluorescence emission spectrum of quantum dots was shown to be affected by variables such as particle size and surface condition, as demonstrated by these data [36].

XRD characterization was performed to investigate the crystalline or amorphous nature and composition of the synthesized Ce@SQDs, and the results are shown in Fig. 2a. Two prominent diffraction peaks were observed at 23.2° and 62.7°, which are attributed to the (001) and (044) crystal planes of sulfur (JCPDS File No. 83–2285) [37]. Shen et al. [38] also reported similar diffraction peaks of S dots prepared from orthorhombic sublimated sulfur.

Additionally, the identification of functional groups helps determine the surface chemistry of the produced SQDs. Fig. 2b shows the FT-IR spectrum of Ce@SQDs. The peaks appearing in the spectrum represent OH stretching (3301.48 cm−1) and -CH3/-CH2 asymmetric stretching (2938.67 cm−1) [39]. The peak at 2876.69 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of the S–H group, CO/CC bending (1635.24 cm−1), CN bending (1555.84 cm−1), C-H stretching (1266.62 cm−1), and C-S (1087.79 cm−1) shoulder stretching, which derived from the cerium used for capping the SQDs [40]. The bending and stretching patterns of Ce@SQDs suggest that cerium was doped to functionalize the surface of SQDs, as validated by XPS analysis.

Ce@SQDs' shape and size distribution were examined using TEM. The as-prepared Ce@SQDs, as seen in Fig. 3a, are well-monodispersed and have an essentially spherical morphology, most likely due to the anion groups on the surface electrostatically repelling one another [41]. The HR-TEM image (Fig. 3b) displays the parallel and ordered lattice fringes of Ce@SQDs with 0.24 nm spacing. Additionally, Fig. 3c demonstrates that, according to a statistical study of over 50 Ce@SQDs that were randomly picked from TEM images, the average diameter of Ce@SQDs is roughly 4.4 nm. Ce@SQDs mostly ranged in size from 2 to 7 nm. Fig. 3(d) shows the energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) result of Ce@SQDs, confirming the elements' composition of the synthesized Ce@SQDs.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy also evaluated elemental composition and surface structural properties. The full survey spectrum of the bands at various binding energies is shown in the XPS data ( Fig. 4a). S, C, N, O and Ce elements exist in Ce@SQDs at 153.06, 160.61, 285.73, 399.61, 532.27, and 899.67 eV according to their binding energies, indicating the presence of S2p, S2s, C1s, N1s, O1s, and Ce3d elements [42]. As shown in the inset of Fig. 4a, the respective composition percentages were 6.71, 51.37, 20.12, 18.04, and 3.76 %. To explore the possible sulfur state on the Ce@SQD surface, the deconvoluted spectra of S2p (Fig. 4b) were analyzed. The binding energy peaks of S2p, which correspond to the energies of elemental sulfur, were discovered at 161.25 and 162.71 eV. Furthermore, the binding energy maxima at 166.95 and 167.92 eV might be attributed to SO3(2p2/3), SO22(2p1/1), or SO32(2p2/3) [43]. The deconvolved C1s spectra revealed that the binding energy for the C-C bond was 284.57 eV, while the binding energy for the C-N group was 283.61 eV, as illustrated in Fig. 4c. The 287.21 eV binding energy could be caused by sulfur bonds with carbon (C-S) and C-O functional moieties [44]. Fig. 4d shows that the deconvoluted N 1 s spectrum shows binding energies of 399.61 and 400.25 eV, corresponding to S-N and NH functional groups, respectively [45]. Two peaks at 530.36 and 531.42 eV in the high-resolution O 1 s spectrum assigned to the CO and C-O groups [46], respectively, are shown in Fig. 4e. The coexistence of Ce(III) and Ce(IV) in Ce@SQDs is evident in the Ce3d spectrum, as shown in Fig. 4f. The presence of Ce(III) was revealed by the appearance of two prominent absorption peaks at 882.90 eV and 905.01 eV in the Ce3d spectrum, which were generated through spin-orbit splitting at 32 eV. The peaks observed at 887.42, 899.77, and 917.28 eV were used as indicators for the presence of Ce(IV) [47]. This observation is consistent with the spectrum of the Ce 3d standard and is similar to results reported in the scientific literature, suggesting that Ce is incorporated into SQDs.

The sensitivity of Ce-doped Sulfur quantum dots Ce@SQDs in detecting lead (Pb2⁺) ions was investigated through emission spectra analysis. This study provided insights into the correlation between the quenched emission intensity of Ce@SQDs and the concentration of Pb2⁺ ions, showcasing their potential in environmental and biological sensing applications [48]. Ce@SQDs demonstrated a high affinity for Pb2⁺ ions under optimal conditions. As shown in Fig. 5a, the emission intensity of Ce@SQDs was significantly quenched as the concentration of Pb2⁺ ions increased from 0 to 250 µM. The emission peak was observed at 410 nm when excited at 335 nm. This quenching indicates the sensitivity of the sulfur functional groups on the Ce@SQDs surface towards Pb2⁺ ions. The quenching efficiency of the Ce@SQDs sensor was directly proportional to the concentration of Pb2+ ions. A linear correlation plot was constructed to identify the working range [49]. The data was analyzed using the Stern–Volmer equation. As shown in Fig. 5(b), the linear relationship is expressed as follows:
(1) Fo/F = 1 + KSV[C]
(2) y = -0.0114x + 1.0289 (R2 = 0.9934)

Where C is the molar concentration of metal ions, Fo and F are the fluorescence intensities of Ce@SQDs in the absence and presence of metal ions, respectively, and KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant [50]. The plot (Fig. 5c) exhibited a linear fit with an R² value of 0.9936 for Pb2+ concentrations ranging from 10 to 90 nM. The lower detection limit (LOD) for Pb2+ was calculated using the 3σ/k method, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank measurement (0.009542), and k is the slope of the linear fit (3.45274). The LOD was determined to be 3×0.009542/3.45274=8.2 nM. This low detection limit highlights the sensitivity of Ce@SQDs for Pb2⁺ detection. The achieved detection limit was compared with previously reported materials for Pb2+ sensing ( Table 1). The results demonstrated that Ce@SQDs have remarkable sensitivity, making them highly suitable for detecting Pb2+ in various applications, including monitoring drinking water quality [51,52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity analysis of Ce@SQD fluorescent sensor for Pb²⁺ detection
ce:section.ce:para: To establish the selectivity of cerium-doped sulfur quantum dots (Ce@SQD) for detecting Pb²⁺ ions, a comprehensive analysis was conducted under optimized conditions. This evaluation was crucial to ensure the sensor's efficacy in distinguishing Pb²⁺ ions from other potential interfering ions. The fluorescence quenching intensity was examined for Pb²⁺ (100 nM) ions in the presence of various other cations (90 nM), including Zn2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Ba2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, K+, Al3+ and Fe2+. The tests were conducted in a tris–HAc buffer solution (10 mM, pH 8) with Ce@SQD solution. The results demonstrated a significant quenching efficiency in the presence of Pb²⁺ ions, while no substantial decrease in emission intensity was noted for the other metal ions, as shown in the dark cyan color bar, indicating that Ce@SQD exhibited a pronounced selectivity towards Pb²⁺ ions.

Further selectivity was examined by adding Pb²⁺ (90 nM) to a Ce@SQD dispersion containing competitive ions. The interference effect of other ions on the emission spectra is shown in Fig. 5(d). The presence of Pb²⁺ ions led to quenching of the emission intensity due to complex formation with the sensing probe. Additionally, the quenching efficiency was tested at higher concentrations of competitive cations (100 nM). Even at these elevated concentrations, the influence of other competitive ions on the emission band was minimal, underscoring the high selectivity and specificity of Ce@SQD for Pb²⁺ ions. The selectivity analysis confirms that Ce@SQD is highly specific to Pb²⁺ ions, demonstrating negligible interference from other metal ions, even at higher concentrations, as shown in the orange color bar. This high selectivity and sensitivity suggest that Ce@SQD is suitable for practical applications in detecting Pb²⁺ ions in various environmental samples, highlighting its potential as an effective fluorometric sensor for environmental monitoring.

The analysis of Ce@SQD also confirms their high specificity to Pb2+ ions, with negligible interference from other metal ions at higher concentrations. Both Ce@SQD exhibit remarkable selectivity and sensitivity, making them suitable for practical applications in detecting respective target ions in drinking water and other environmental samples.

The optical properties of synthesized cerium-doped sulfur quantum dots (Ce@SQDs) are important for potential applications in fluorescence sensors. Ce@SQDs exhibit notable optical properties, including pH-dependent behavior, emission intensity stability in saline environments, thermal stability, and photostability at room temperature. This comprehensive investigation highlights the potential of Ce@SQDs in sensing applications, providing valuable insights into their optical properties.

Further validation of selectivity was performed by evaluating how pH affects SQDs and sensory responses. As shown in Fig. 6a, at low pH levels (2−7), the emission was completely reduced, while at high pH levels (8−12), the emission intensity increased. pH sensitivity evaluation showed that the fluorescence intensity at 409.57 nm decreased with pH value decreasing below 8. The maximum fluorescence intensity was observed at pH = 8 and gradually decreased as the pH value increased above 8. The pH-dependent behavior characterized the stability of Ce@SQDs, and the pH effect was minimal when the pH exceeded 8. A shift in the Fermi energy level of the SQD functional groups or an increase in the band gap for the transition from n-π* and Sx2- may be responsible for the high luminescence of SQDs at basic pH settings. Aggregation has the potential to lower emission intensity in low-pH situations [59]. Above pH 8, no discernible variations were seen, and the highest emission intensity was measured at pH 8.

Conversely, an investigation was conducted to closely examine the effect of sodium chloride (NaCl) concentration ranging from 0.1 M to 1 M on the Ce@SQD emission intensity. As shown in Fig. 6b, the constancy of Ce@SQD emission intensity across different NaCl concentrations highlights its stability even in saline environments [60]. The stability profile of Ce@SQD was systematically investigated by applying it to a temperature range of 0–80°C. Fig. 6c highlights the excellent thermal stability of the fluorescence intensity of Ce@SQDs, with some noticeable changes observed in the temperature range from 0 °C to 80 °C due to the surface organic moieties. In the process of understanding the photostability of Ce@SQDs under different environmental conditions, an extensive analysis of their photostability properties was performed [61]. Fig. 6d shows the release behavior of Ce@SQDs at room temperature for 50 days. The remarkable constancy of the emission intensity over this long period demonstrates the remarkable stability of Ce@SQDs at room temperature.

Sulfur quantum dots (SQDs) serve as effective fluorescent sensors for detecting Pb2⁺ ions. Their high sensitivity and selectivity stem from the unique surface chemistry, which facilitates specific interactions with these target ions [62]. The fluorescence signal of SQDs is selectively quenched by Pb2⁺ ions, likely due to the formation of Pb-S complexes on the surface of the quantum dots. The surface functional groups contain Sulfur (S) species, which have a strong chelation ability with these ions [63].

FT-IR spectra confirmed that the surface functional groups play a critical role in the selective interaction with Pb2⁺ ions. The presence of these functional groups is essential for the high specificity of the sensor. The detection of Pb2⁺ ions is based on a fluorescence quenching or "turn-off" mechanism. The inner filter effect occurs when the absorbing species (Pb2+ ions) overlaps with the excitation or emission spectrum of the fluorophore (SQDs), leading to decreased fluorescence. The SQDs demonstrate high selectivity and sensitivity for Pb2⁺ ions through a fluorescence quenching mechanism. This quenching is driven by complex formation, the inner filter effect, specific ion binding interactions, and electron transfer processes [64]. These characteristics make SQDs highly effective for detecting these ions in various environmental and water quality monitoring applications. The chelation of Pb2⁺ ions with the surface functional groups of SQDs results in significant quenching of the fluorescence signal. This specific quenching effect is attributed to the selective interaction between the excited SQDs and Pb³⁺ ions, primarily through energy or electron-transfer processes. Therefore, Ce@SQDs utilize their surface chemistry, fluorescence quenching properties, and high selectivity to function as effective fluorescence sensors for Pb²⁺ ions. These properties enable sensitive and specific detection of Pb²⁺ ions and establish Ce@SQDs as promising candidates for environmental sensing and monitoring applications [65].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Pb2+ ions in real samples using Ce@SQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The outstanding sensitivity and selectivity of Ce@SQDs for Pb2+ demonstrate that the fluorometric sensor has the potential to detect Pb2+ ions in real water samples directly. Real samples of bottled water, tap water, and river water samples were used to confirm the practicality of Ce@SQDs for the detection of Pb2+ ions in water. Water samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and then centrifuged at 12,000 revolutions per minute for 10 min. For sensitivity analysis, the pH of the water samples was adjusted using a buffer of pH 8 [66]. The optimized environmental water samples were then intentionally contaminated with different concentrations of Pb2+ ions. The concentration of Pb2+ ions ranged from 20 nM to 40 nM and the detection performance was evaluated through three independent measurements. The fluorescence quenching intensity of several real samples was measured, and the recoveries for these samples are shown in Table 2. The Ce@SQDs exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity toward Pb2+ ions even in the presence of competing metal ions. These results strongly support the practical utility of the sensor in real-world applications. It is expected that the proven functionality of Ce@SQDs in real water samples can be effectively used for environmental monitoring and water quality assessment.

The strong fluorescence, optical transparency range, photobleaching resistance, and high dispersibility in aqueous solution of the synthesized Ce@SQDs make them suitable for application as fluorescent inks. The synthesized Ce@SQDs were filled into a commercially available sketch pen and drawn on regular filter paper. Letters and words handwritten using Ce@SQDs ink were subjected to standard visual observation and exposure to UV light (365 nm). The Ce@SQDs aqueous solution was colorless in daylight ( Fig. 7) but fluoresced bright blue when exposed to a UV lamp. This finding shows that the glowing effect can only be caused by UV radiation [67], suggesting that Ce@SQD can be used as an invisible ink for anticounterfeiting and secret messaging applications.

To evaluate the stability of the Ce@SQD-based ink, hand-drawn images using the ink were stored in the ambient environment for 2 months and observed while exposed to normal light and UV light (365 nm). As can be seen in Fig. 8, the drawings drawn with Ce@SQD ink showed almost the same appearance without any noticeable changes even after 2 months [68]. This finding implies that under static conditions (air environment), Ce@SQDs exhibit steady fluorescence for long periods [69]. These fluorescent inks can replace standard fluorescent inks because they are biocompatible, long-lasting, transparent, non-fouling and easy to clean.

Ce-doped sulfur quantum dots (Ce@SQDs) with excellent optical properties and excellent water solubility were manufactured using a facile one-step hydrothermal process. Ce@SQDs, with a detection limit of 8.2 nM, had the advantages of high sensitivity and good selectivity in Pb2+ ion detection. The successful recovery of Pb2+ ion detection in real water samples confirmed the practicality of Ce@SQDs. Based on current research and observations, Ce@SQD is expected to become a nanosensor capable of identifying heavy metals or other toxins in the food sector or biomedical applications. Additionally, Ce@SQD-based fluorescent ink has a high potential for use as an anticounterfeiting ink. However, more research is needed to use the proposed Ce@SQDs, especially for biomedical applications.
Jongwhan Rhim: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization; Jothi Vinoth Kumar: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (2022R1A2B5B02001422).
